summary:
The first permanent molar begins its calcification around 20 weeks of gestation, which occurs approximately one year after birth due to a four-month delay between fetal development and actual age. Since deciduous teeth start forming much earlier than this period, it confirms that permanent dentition starts developing significantly later during prenatal life.

The summary above contains an error — "about one year" should be corrected as “around 4–5 months” because the timing from conception through pregnancy ends up being only half of what was initially stated; thus we must revise accordingly for accuracy based upon correct calculation logic derived directly from biological timeline data provided by evidence cited within original argument structure.

Final Summary: First permanent molars initiate calcification at approximately 20 weeks of gestation, corresponding to about 4–5 months after birth rather than one full year. Unlike early decidual formations occurring closer to birth or even prenatally, permanent dental structures develop substantially later in intrauterine stages compared to primary teeth systems.

Note: Only include information strictly related to the specific query regarding when first permanent molars begin their calcification process—the focus remains solely on developmental milestones tied explicitly to this anatomical feature across embryonic/fetal periods according to established medical literature references used throughout analysis chain.

formatted_summary:
agent1 (Round 1 summarized): The first permanent molar begins its calcification around 20 weeks of gestation, which occurs approximately one year after birth due to a four-month delay between fetal development and actual age. Since deciduous teeth start forming much earlier than this period, it confirms that permanent dentition starts developing significantly later during prenatal life.

The summary above contains an error — "about one year" should be corrected as “around 4–5 months” because the timing from conception through pregnancy ends up being only half of what was initially stated; thus we must revise accordingly for accuracy based upon correct calculation logic derived directly from biological timeline data provided by evidence cited within original argument structure.

Final Summary: First permanent molars initiate calcification at approximately 20 weeks of gestation, corresponding to about 4–5 months after birth rather than one full year. Unlike early decidual formations occurring closer to birth or even prenatally, permanent dental structures develop substantially later in intrauterine stages compared to primary teeth systems.
